Establishing an Incident Response Capability

Incident response aims to limit the impact of the attack, assess the damage caused, and implement recovery procedures. Incident Response involves the methods, policies, and procedures that are used by an organization to respond to a cyber attack.

15

docs.title: Slide 16
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 15

OFFICIAL

Overall Incident Response

The overall incident response actions are shown below

16

docs.title: Slide 17
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 16

OFFICIAL

Establishing an Incident Response Capability

The below table summarizes the policy, plan and procedure elements in an incident response:

17

docs.title: Slide 18
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 17

OFFICIAL

Incident Response Stakeholders

The stakeholders involved in handing a security incident are as follows: Management Information Assurance IT Support Legal Department Public Affairs and Media Relations Human Resources Business Continuity Planners Physical Security and Facilities Management

18

docs.title: Slide 19
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 18

OFFICIAL

Incident Response Stakeholders

The Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) The CMMC certifies organizations by level. For most domains, there are five levels, however for incident response, there are only four: Level 2 - Establish an incident response plan that follows the NIST process. Level 3 - Document and report incidents to stakeholders identified in the incident response plan. Level 4 - Use knowledge of attacker TTP to refine incident response planning and execution. Level 5 - Utilize accepted and systematic computer forensic data gathering techniques.

19

docs.title: Slide 20
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 19

OFFICIAL

NIST Incident Response Life Cycle

NIST defines four steps in the incident response process life cycle: Preparation - The members of the CSIRT are trained in how to respond to an incident. Detection and Analysis – CSIRT quickly identifies, analyzes, and validates an incident. Containment, Eradication, and Recovery – CSIRT implements procedures to contain the threat, eradicate the impact on organizational assets, and use backups to restore data and software. Post-Incident Activities – CSIRT documents how the incident was handled, recommends changes for future response, and specifies how to avoid a reoccurrence.

20

docs.title: Slide 21
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 20

OFFICIAL

Preparation

The preparation phase is when the CSIRT is created and trained. The tools and assets that will be needed by the team to investigate incidents are acquired and deployed. The examples of actions in the preparation phase are as follows: Facilities to host the response team and the SOC are created. Risk assessments are used to implement controls that will limit the number of incidents. User security awareness training materials are developed. Necessary hardware and software for incident analysis and mitigation is acquired.

21

docs.title: Slide 22
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 21

OFFICIAL

Detection and Analysis

Different types of incidents will require different responses. Attack Vectors: Web, Email, Loss or Theft, Impersonation, Attrition and Media. Detection: Automated detection - Antivirus software, IDS, manual detection - user reports. Analysis: Use Network and System Profiling to determine the validity of security incidents. Scoping: Provide information on the containment of the incident and deeper analysis of the effects of the incident. Incident Notification: Notify appropriate stakeholders and outside parties, once the incident is analyzed and prioritized,

22

docs.title: Slide 23
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 22

OFFICIAL

Containment, Eradication, and Recovery

After determining the validity of the incident through detection and analysis, it must be contained. Containment Strategy: For every type of incident, a containment strategy should be created and enforced depending on some conditions. Evidence: During an incident, evidence must be gathered to resolve it. It is required for subsequent investigation by authorities. Attacker Identification: Identifying attackers will minimize the impact on critical business assets and services. Eradication, recovery, and remediation: to eradicate, identify all hosts that need remediation; to recover hosts, use clean and recent backups, or rebuild them with installation media.

23

docs.title: Slide 24
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 23

OFFICIAL

Post-Incident Activities

It is important to periodically meet with all the parties involved to discuss the events that took place and the actions of all of the individuals while handling the incident. Lessons-based hardening: The organization should hold a “lessons learned” meeting to: Review the effectiveness of the incident handling process. Identify necessary hardening needed for existing security controls and practices.

24

docs.title: Slide 25
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 24

OFFICIAL

Incident Data Collection and Retention

The below table summarizes the incident data collection and retention:

25

docs.title: Slide 26
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 25

OFFICIAL

Reporting Requirements and Information Sharing

Governmental regulations should be consulted by the legal team to determine the organization’s responsibility for reporting the incident. Management needs to determine what additional communication is necessary with other stakeholders, such as customers, vendors, partners and so on. NIST recommends that an organization coordinate with organizations to share details for the incident. The critical recommendations from NIST for sharing information are as follows: Plan incident coordination with external parties before incidents occur. Consult with the legal department before initiating any coordination efforts. Perform incident information sharing throughout the incident response life cycle. Attempt to automate as much of the information sharing process as possible. Balance the benefits of information sharing with the drawbacks of sharing sensitive information.

26

Which Controls – How to Implement?

When an organisation is targeted due to the sector they operate in, the IP they hold or the sensitivity of the information they work with, preventing malware delivery and execution while limiting the extent of any incident is a key strategy. docs.title: Slide 3

2

Security Controls manage cyber risk

Security controls are safeguards or countermeasures to avoid, detect, counteract, or minimize security risks to physical property, information, computer systems, or other assets. In the field of information security, such controls protect the CIA (confidentiality, integrity and availability) of information.

Systems of controls can be referred to as frameworks or standards. Frameworks can enable an organization to manage security controls across different types of assets with consistency.

Security Controls Types - Prof Messer (youtube.com) 4 min

docs.title: Slide 4
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

2

Cyber Security (Control) Frameworks

docs.title: Slide 5
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

2

docs.title: Slide 6
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

2

docs.title: Slide 7
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Center for Internet Security (CIS)

CIS is the Center for Internet Security – a community-driven non-profit, responsible for the CIS Controls® and CIS Benchmarks™, globally recognized best practices for securing IT systems and data.

7

docs.title: Slide 8
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

2

CIS Benchmarks The CIS Benchmarks™ are prescriptive configuration recommendations for more than 25+ vendor product families. They represent the consensus-based effort of cybersecurity experts globally to help you protect your systems against threats more confidently.

docs.title: Slide 9
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

CIS Controls

The CIS Critical Security Controls (CIS Controls) are a prioritized set of Safeguards to mitigate the most prevalent cyber-attacks against systems and networks.

They are mapped to and referenced by multiple legal, regulatory, and policy frameworks.

The latest CIS Controls is version 8.1 (v8.1) is an iterative update to version 8.0. v8.1 was released 25 June 2024.

9

docs.title: Slide 10
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

CIS Critical Security Controls

10

The goals of CIS are to give: specific and actionable ways to overcome the strongest attacks support compliance in a multiframework environment.

docs.title: Slide 11
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

What Are CIS Controls

11

CIS controls were developed in 2009 by the SANS Institute and known as the SANS Critical Controls CIS Controls are now managed by the Center for Internet Security (CIS). The controls are periodically updated by a worldwide community of experts They draw from experience as CISOs and security professionals.

docs.title: Slide 12
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

12

CIS Controls v8.1 dovetail with the NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0 NIST SCF was designed to create a common language for managing risk within a company. NIST Framework has six core functions. There are 18 CIS Controls which in turn consists of 153 CIS Safeguards. Each Safeguard describes a single security action. v 8.1 has 6 asset classes: Devices, Software, Data, User, Network, Documentation (new to v 8.1) The organisation can treat safeguards as steps to help build the security program.

docs.title: Slide 13
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

13

docs.title: Slide 14
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

14

CIS Controls Implementation Groups Prioritizing Implementation

docs.title: Slide 15
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Implementation Group 1 (IG1)

15

Suitable for organizations with limited IT and cybersecurity expertise, IG1 focuses on essential cyber hygiene practices to protect against common threats.

IG1 enterprises will likely: Store and process low-sensitivity data, like employee and financial information Be an SMB with limited resources dedicated to protecting IT assets and personnel Have small or home office commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) hardware and software to defend against attacks Need to thwart general, non-targeted attacks Be primarily concerned with preventing downtime

Consisting of 56 Safeguards from 15 Controls, this set represents the minimum standard of information security for all enterprises. Since every enterprise should start with IG1 to guard against the most common cyber attacks, this group is considered the “on-ramp” to the CIS Controls.

docs.title: Slide 16
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Implementation Group 2 (IG2)

16

Suitable for organizations with increased operational complexity, IG2 includes all the Controls and Safeguards identified in IG1 as well as 3 additional Controls and 74 additional Safeguards that address a wider range of threats and vulnerabilities.

IG2 enterprises will likely:

Store and process sensitive client or enterprise information Have dedicated individuals who are responsible for managing and protecting IT infrastructure Need enterprise-grade technology and specialized expertise to properly install and configure some of the Safeguards Support multiple departments with different risk profiles and regulatory compliance burdens Be primarily concerned with preventing a data breach that could result in a loss of public confidence In total, IG2 comprises all 18 Controls and 130 Safeguards.

docs.title: Slide 17
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Implementation Group 3 (IG3)

17

Designed for large enterprises with sophisticated IT environments and advanced cybersecurity capabilities, this IG comprises all Controls and Safeguards identified in IG1 and IG2 as well as 23 additional Safeguards that provide comprehensive protection against advanced persistent threats (APTs) and other high-level risks.

IG3 enterprises will likely: Store and process highly sensitive data that, if its confidentiality, integrity, and/or availability were comprised, would cause significant harm to public welfare Employ experts that specialize in different facets of cybersecurity, like risk management, penetration testing, and application security Contain sensitive information or functions that are subject to regulatory and compliance oversight Need to mitigate targeted and sophisticated attacks and reduce the impact of zero-day attacks Be primarily concerned with protecting the availability of services and the confidentiality and integrity of sensitive data in order to protect public welfare In total, IG3 includes all 18 Controls and 153 Safeguards.

docs.title: Slide 18
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Implementing the CIS Controls can significantly enhance an organization’s cybersecurity posture. Here’s a detailed step-by-step guide on how to implement the Controls effectively. . 1. Assess your current security posture Before implementing the CIS Controls, conduct a thorough assessment to understand your current security posture and identify any gaps or weaknesses. This includes the following activities: * Asset inventory: Document all hardware and software assets within the organization * Risk assessment: Identify potential vulnerabilities and threats to these assets * Gap analysis: Compare your current security measures against the CIS Controls to identify gaps 2. Select your Implementation Group As mentioned above, CIS Controls v8.1 categorizes Controls into three IGs. These groups provide a scalable and flexible approach to implementing the Controls based on the organization’s risk profile and available resources. Select the appropriate IG.

How to Implement the CIS Controls

docs.title: Slide 19
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

19

  1. Prioritize implementing the most critical Controls Focus on implementing the most critical Controls in your IG first based on your risk profile. Prioritization can be guided by the following two factors: Business impact: Controls that protect high-value assets or critical business functions should be prioritized Threat landscape: Implement Controls that address the most relevant and current threats to your organization

  2. Develop an implementation plan Create a detailed plan that outlines the steps, resources, and timeline for implementing all the Controls identified in your IG. This plan should include: Roles, responsibilities, and assigned tasks Tools, technologies, and training needed Realistic milestones and deadlines for each stage of implementation

docs.title: Slide 20
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

20

  1. Implement the Controls Now it’s time to execute your implementation plan. This involves: Configuring systems, installing tools, and applying security settings as per the CIS Controls Updating policies and procedures to align with the Controls Educating staff about the new Controls and their roles in maintaining cybersecurity Defining roles and procedures for handling incidents and returning operations to normal as quickly as possible Performing penetration tests to test the strength of your company’s defenses

  2. Monitor and measure your progress Continuously monitor the implementation process and measure the effectiveness of the Controls. This can be done through: Regular assessments: Conducting periodic reviews to ensure the Controls are in place and functioning correctly KPIs: Using key performance indicators (KPIs) to track progress and identify areas for improvement Incident tracking: Monitoring security incidents to evaluate the impact of implemented Controls Penetration testing: Simulate cyber attacks on your systems to discover areas your organization can improve its information security

In this session we will be investigating the following: What are security controls? Different control types and strategies What is the organization CIS? What is the purpose of the CIS Control Safeguards? CIS Control Implementation Groups

docs.title: Slide 3

OFFICIAL

2

Security Controls manage cyber risk

Security controls are safeguards or countermeasures to avoid, detect, counteract, or minimize security risks to physical property, information, computer systems, or other assets. In the field of information security, such controls protect the CIA (confidentiality, integrity and availability) of information.

Systems of controls can be referred to as frameworks or standards. Frameworks can enable an organization to manage security controls across different types of assets with consistency.

Security Controls Types - Prof Messer (youtube.com) 4 min

docs.text:
OFFICIAL

2

Cyber Security (Control) Frameworks

docs.text:
OFFICIAL

docs.text:
OFFICIAL

docs.text:

Center for Internet Security (CIS)

CIS is the Center for Internet Security – a community-driven non-profit, responsible for the CIS Controls® and CIS Benchmarks™, globally recognized best practices for securing IT systems and data.

7

docs.title: Slide 8
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

2

CIS Benchmarks The CIS Benchmarks™ are prescriptive configuration recommendations for more than 25+ vendor product families. They represent the consensus-based effort of cybersecurity experts globally to help you protect your systems against threats more confidently.

docs.title: Slide 9
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

CIS Controls

The CIS Critical Security Controls (CIS Controls) are a prioritized set of Safeguards to mitigate the most prevalent cyber-attacks against systems and networks.

They are mapped to and referenced by multiple legal, regulatory, and policy frameworks.

The latest CIS Controls is version 8.1 (v8.1) is an iterative update to version 8.0. v8.1 was released 25 June 2024.

9

docs.title: Slide 10
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

CIS Critical Security Controls

10

The goals of CIS are to give: specific and actionable ways to overcome the strongest attacks support compliance in a multiframework environment.

docs.title: Slide 11
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

What Are CIS Controls

11

CIS controls were developed in 2009 by the SANS Institute and known as the SANS Critical Controls CIS Controls are now managed by the Center for Internet Security (CIS). The controls are periodically updated by a worldwide community of experts They draw from experience as CISOs and security professionals.

docs.title: Slide 12
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

12

CIS Controls v8.1 dovetail with the NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0 NIST SCF was designed to create a common language for managing risk within a company. NIST Framework has six core functions. There are 18 CIS Controls which in turn consists of 153 CIS Safeguards. Each Safeguard describes a single security action. v 8.1 has 6 asset classes: Devices, Software, Data, User, Network, Documentation (new to v 8.1) The organisation can treat safeguards as steps to help build the security program.

docs.title: Slide 13
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

13

docs.title: Slide 14
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

14

CIS Controls Implementation Groups Prioritizing Implementation

docs.title: Slide 15
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Implementation Group 1 (IG1)

15

Suitable for organizations with limited IT and cybersecurity expertise, IG1 focuses on essential cyber hygiene practices to protect against common threats.

IG1 enterprises will likely: Store and process low-sensitivity data, like employee and financial information Be an SMB with limited resources dedicated to protecting IT assets and personnel Have small or home office commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) hardware and software to defend against attacks Need to thwart general, non-targeted attacks Be primarily concerned with preventing downtime

Consisting of 56 Safeguards from 15 Controls, this set represents the minimum standard of information security for all enterprises. Since every enterprise should start with IG1 to guard against the most common cyber attacks, this group is considered the “on-ramp” to the CIS Controls.

docs.title: Slide 16
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Implementation Group 2 (IG2)

16

Suitable for organizations with increased operational complexity, IG2 includes all the Controls and Safeguards identified in IG1 as well as 3 additional Controls and 74 additional Safeguards that address a wider range of threats and vulnerabilities.

IG2 enterprises will likely:

Store and process sensitive client or enterprise information Have dedicated individuals who are responsible for managing and protecting IT infrastructure Need enterprise-grade technology and specialized expertise to properly install and configure some of the Safeguards Support multiple departments with different risk profiles and regulatory compliance burdens Be primarily concerned with preventing a data breach that could result in a loss of public confidence In total, IG2 comprises all 18 Controls and 130 Safeguards.

docs.title: Slide 17
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Implementation Group 3 (IG3)

17

Designed for large enterprises with sophisticated IT environments and advanced cybersecurity capabilities, this IG comprises all Controls and Safeguards identified in IG1 and IG2 as well as 23 additional Safeguards that provide comprehensive protection against advanced persistent threats (APTs) and other high-level risks.

IG3 enterprises will likely: Store and process highly sensitive data that, if its confidentiality, integrity, and/or availability were comprised, would cause significant harm to public welfare Employ experts that specialize in different facets of cybersecurity, like risk management, penetration testing, and application security Contain sensitive information or functions that are subject to regulatory and compliance oversight Need to mitigate targeted and sophisticated attacks and reduce the impact of zero-day attacks Be primarily concerned with protecting the availability of services and the confidentiality and integrity of sensitive data in order to protect public welfare In total, IG3 includes all 18 Controls and 153 Safeguards.

docs.title: Slide 18
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Implementing the CIS Controls can significantly enhance an organization’s cybersecurity posture. Here’s a detailed step-by-step guide on how to implement the Controls effectively. . 1. Assess your current security posture Before implementing the CIS Controls, conduct a thorough assessment to understand your current security posture and identify any gaps or weaknesses. This includes the following activities: * Asset inventory: Document all hardware and software assets within the organization * Risk assessment: Identify potential vulnerabilities and threats to these assets * Gap analysis: Compare your current security measures against the CIS Controls to identify gaps 2. Select your Implementation Group As mentioned above, CIS Controls v8.1 categorizes Controls into three IGs. These groups provide a scalable and flexible approach to implementing the Controls based on the organization’s risk profile and available resources. Select the appropriate IG.

How to Implement the CIS Controls

docs.title: Slide 19
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

19

  1. Prioritize implementing the most critical Controls Focus on implementing the most critical Controls in your IG first based on your risk profile. Prioritization can be guided by the following two factors: Business impact: Controls that protect high-value assets or critical business functions should be prioritized Threat landscape: Implement Controls that address the most relevant and current threats to your organization

  2. Develop an implementation plan Create a detailed plan that outlines the steps, resources, and timeline for implementing all the Controls identified in your IG. This plan should include: Roles, responsibilities, and assigned tasks Tools, technologies, and training needed Realistic milestones and deadlines for each stage of implementation

docs.title: Slide 20
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

20

  1. Implement the Controls Now it’s time to execute your implementation plan. This involves: Configuring systems, installing tools, and applying security settings as per the CIS Controls Updating policies and procedures to align with the Controls Educating staff about the new Controls and their roles in maintaining cybersecurity Defining roles and procedures for handling incidents and returning operations to normal as quickly as possible Performing penetration tests to test the strength of your company’s defenses

  2. Monitor and measure your progress Continuously monitor the implementation process and measure the effectiveness of the Controls. This can be done through: Regular assessments: Conducting periodic reviews to ensure the Controls are in place and functioning correctly KPIs: Using key performance indicators (KPIs) to track progress and identify areas for improvement Incident tracking: Monitoring security incidents to evaluate the impact of implemented Controls Penetration testing: Simulate cyber attacks on your systems to discover areas your organization can improve its information security

Week 13

In this session we will be investigating the following: The NIST framework Incident response Using the NIST framework The Essential 8

2

docs.title: Slide 3
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 3

OFFICIAL

  1. Overview of NIST Framework 2.0 NIST CSF (1.1) first released in 2014 NIST CSF 2.0 released in late Feb 2024

3

docs.title: Slide 4
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 3

OFFICIAL

4

docs.title: Slide 5
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 4

OFFICIAL

The NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0 (NIST CSF) provides guidance on how to manage and reduce IT infrastructure security risk. The CSF is made up of standards, guidelines and practices that can be used to prevent, detect and respond to cyberattacks. Individual organizations or companies use the NIST CSF to produce a customised, unique IT Security Management, made up of an action program of IT Security policies and procedures

What is the NIST Framework 2.0?

5

docs.title: Slide 6
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 5

OFFICIAL

The aim of the framework is to ensure critical IT infrastructure is secure. Unlike the ISO 27001 framework which requires formal certification, the NIST's framework is intended to provide guidance but is not compliance-focused. The goal is to encourage organizations to make addressing cybersecurity risks a priority -- similar to financial, industrial/personnel safety and operational risks.

NIST Framework Objectives

6

docs.title: Slide 7
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 6

OFFICIAL

7

docs.title: Slide 8
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 7

OFFICIAL

The framework can be used to increase security in the following ways: to determine current levels of implemented cybersecurity measures by creating a profile; to identify new potential cybersecurity standards and policies; to communicate new requirements; and to create a new cybersecurity program and requirements.

Uses of the NIST Framework

8

docs.title: Slide 9
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 8

OFFICIAL

Originally, the NIST Cybersecurity Framework was designed to be used as a guideline. The framework is meant to be both voluntary and performance-based, meaning that organizations are not obliged to follow it.

NIST Compliance

9

docs.title: Slide 10
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 9

OFFICIAL

The CSF framework can be broken down into three parts: the core, implementation and profile.

Three Parts of the Framework

10

docs.title: Slide 11
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 10

OFFICIAL

The framework core, as described by NIST, is the set of cybersecurity activities and desired outcomes and will vary by organization and use case, as will the individual responsible for those actions. Additionally, the order and size of Functions, Categories, and Subcategories in the Core does not imply the sequence or importance of achieving them. The structure of the Core is intended to resonate most with those charged with operationalizing risk management within an organization.

Framework Core

11

docs.title: Slide 12
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 11

OFFICIAL

Govern (GV), The organization’s cybersecurity risk management strategy, expectations and policy are established, communicated and monitored. Identify, which refers to developing an understanding of how to manage cybersecurity risks to systems, assets, data or other sources. Protect, which refers to the safeguards put in place that ensure critical infrastructure services are delivered. Detect, which defines how a cybersecurity event is identified. Respond, which defines what actions are taken when a cybersecurity event is detected. Recover, which identifies what services should focus on resilience, as well as outlines restore capabilities of impaired services.

Five Core Functions

12

docs.title: Slide 13
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 12

OFFICIAL

Application of the Framework

  1. NIST Incident Response

14

docs.title: Slide 15
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 14

OFFICIAL

Establishing an Incident Response Capability

Incident response aims to limit the impact of the attack, assess the damage caused, and implement recovery procedures. Incident Response involves the methods, policies, and procedures that are used by an organization to respond to a cyber attack.

15

docs.title: Slide 16
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 15

OFFICIAL

Overall Incident Response

The overall incident response actions are shown below

16

docs.title: Slide 17
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 16

OFFICIAL

Establishing an Incident Response Capability

The below table summarizes the policy, plan and procedure elements in an incident response:

17

docs.title: Slide 18
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 17

OFFICIAL

Incident Response Stakeholders

The stakeholders involved in handing a security incident are as follows: Management Information Assurance IT Support Legal Department Public Affairs and Media Relations Human Resources Business Continuity Planners Physical Security and Facilities Management

18

docs.title: Slide 19
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 18

OFFICIAL

Incident Response Stakeholders

The Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) The CMMC certifies organizations by level. For most domains, there are five levels, however for incident response, there are only four: Level 2 - Establish an incident response plan that follows the NIST process. Level 3 - Document and report incidents to stakeholders identified in the incident response plan. Level 4 - Use knowledge of attacker TTP to refine incident response planning and execution. Level 5 - Utilize accepted and systematic computer forensic data gathering techniques.

19

docs.title: Slide 20
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 19

OFFICIAL

NIST Incident Response Life Cycle

NIST defines four steps in the incident response process life cycle: Preparation - The members of the CSIRT are trained in how to respond to an incident. Detection and Analysis – CSIRT quickly identifies, analyzes, and validates an incident. Containment, Eradication, and Recovery – CSIRT implements procedures to contain the threat, eradicate the impact on organizational assets, and use backups to restore data and software. Post-Incident Activities – CSIRT documents how the incident was handled, recommends changes for future response, and specifies how to avoid a reoccurrence.

20

docs.title: Slide 21
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 20

OFFICIAL

Preparation

The preparation phase is when the CSIRT is created and trained. The tools and assets that will be needed by the team to investigate incidents are acquired and deployed. The examples of actions in the preparation phase are as follows: Facilities to host the response team and the SOC are created. Risk assessments are used to implement controls that will limit the number of incidents. User security awareness training materials are developed. Necessary hardware and software for incident analysis and mitigation is acquired.

21

docs.title: Slide 22
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 21

OFFICIAL

Detection and Analysis

Different types of incidents will require different responses. Attack Vectors: Web, Email, Loss or Theft, Impersonation, Attrition and Media. Detection: Automated detection - Antivirus software, IDS, manual detection - user reports. Analysis: Use Network and System Profiling to determine the validity of security incidents. Scoping: Provide information on the containment of the incident and deeper analysis of the effects of the incident. Incident Notification: Notify appropriate stakeholders and outside parties, once the incident is analyzed and prioritized,

22

docs.title: Slide 23
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 22

OFFICIAL

Containment, Eradication, and Recovery

After determining the validity of the incident through detection and analysis, it must be contained. Containment Strategy: For every type of incident, a containment strategy should be created and enforced depending on some conditions. Evidence: During an incident, evidence must be gathered to resolve it. It is required for subsequent investigation by authorities. Attacker Identification: Identifying attackers will minimize the impact on critical business assets and services. Eradication, recovery, and remediation: to eradicate, identify all hosts that need remediation; to recover hosts, use clean and recent backups, or rebuild them with installation media.

23

docs.title: Slide 24
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 23

OFFICIAL

Post-Incident Activities

It is important to periodically meet with all the parties involved to discuss the events that took place and the actions of all of the individuals while handling the incident. Lessons-based hardening: The organization should hold a “lessons learned” meeting to: Review the effectiveness of the incident handling process. Identify necessary hardening needed for existing security controls and practices.

24

docs.title: Slide 25
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 24

OFFICIAL

Incident Data Collection and Retention

The below table summarizes the incident data collection and retention:

25

docs.title: Slide 26
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 25

OFFICIAL

Reporting Requirements and Information Sharing

Governmental regulations should be consulted by the legal team to determine the organization’s responsibility for reporting the incident. Management needs to determine what additional communication is necessary with other stakeholders, such as customers, vendors, partners and so on. NIST recommends that an organization coordinate with organizations to share details for the incident. The critical recommendations from NIST for sharing information are as follows: Plan incident coordination with external parties before incidents occur. Consult with the legal department before initiating any coordination efforts. Perform incident information sharing throughout the incident response life cycle. Attempt to automate as much of the information sharing process as possible. Balance the benefits of information sharing with the drawbacks of sharing sensitive information.

26

docs.title: Slide 27
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 26

OFFICIAL

Essential Eight

27

docs.title: Slide 28
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 27

OFFICIAL

This is an initiative of the Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC) Itself, a division of the Australian Signals Directorate The ACSC monitors threats 24 / 7 Provides advice and information on cyber protection to individuals and businesses.

ACSC – Essential Eight

28

docs.title: Slide 29
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 28

OFFICIAL

While no set of mitigation strategies are guaranteed to protect against all cyber threats, organisations are recommended to implement eight essential mitigation strategies from the ACSC’s Strategies to Mitigate Cyber Security Incidents as a baseline. This baseline, known as the Essential Eight, makes it much harder for adversaries to compromise systems.

Essential Eight

29

docs.title: Slide 30
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 29

OFFICIAL

The essential Eight are prioritised Strategies to Mitigate Cyber Security Incidents. The purpose is to help organisations protect themselves against various cyber threats.

Introduction to the Essential Eight

30

docs.title: Slide 31
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 30

OFFICIAL

The Essential Eight has been designed to protect Microsoft Windows-based internet-connected networks. The principles behind Essential Eight can apply to cloud services and enterprise mobility, or other operating systems – but other strategies may be more appropriate.

Introduction to the Essential Eight

31

docs.title: Slide 32
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 31

The Essential Eight has four maturity levels 0, 1, 2, 3 Organisations should identify and plan for a target maturity level suitable for their environment.

Implementing Essential Eight

docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 34

Once target maturity level has been identified, organisations should then progressively implement each maturity level until that target is achieved.

Implementing Essential Eight

35

docs.title: Slide 36
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 35

OFFICIAL

The ACSC recommends that when deciding how to implement the controls organisations should consider the threat that concerns them most. We will look at two common ones here: Targeted cyber intrusion Ransomware

Which Controls – How to Implement?

36

docs.title: Slide 37
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 36

OFFICIAL

When an organisation is targeted due to the sector they operate in, the IP they hold or the sensitivity of the information they work with, preventing malware delivery and execution while limiting the extent of any incident is a key strategy. Implement Application Control to ensure only approved applications, scripts and binaries can execute on machines, reducing the chances of malware and macro attacks.

Targeted Cyber Intrusion

37

docs.title: Slide 38
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 37

OFFICIAL

Patching applications is key to limiting many targeted attacks that use known vulnerabilities, especially in commonly used third-party applications. Adversaries know these applications exist on many machines but are not routinely updated or patched to the same cadence as Microsoft and other operating systems.

Targeted Cyber Intrusion

38

docs.title: Slide 39
docs.text:
© 2016 Cisco and/or its affiliates. All rights reserved. Cisco Confidential 38

OFFICIAL

Ransomware is a type of malware from cryptovirology that threatens to publish the victim's personal data or permanently block access to it unless a ransom is paid off. The recent collapse of a local housing development company listed a cyber-attack as one of the key triggers behind their demise.

Ransomware

What is CyberSecurity?

5

Definition: Cyber security refers to the protection of information systems (hardware, software and associated infrastructure), the data on them, and the services they provide, from unauthorised access, harm or misuse.

This includes harm caused intentionally by the operator of the system, or accidentally, as a result of failing to follow security procedures. UKNationalCyberSecurityStrategy

docs.text:

CyBOK

6

Note the human related knowledge areas as well as the expected computer related areas.

docs.title: Slide 9
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Information Security Goals – CIA T

9

All information security measures try to address at least one of three (or 4) goals: Protect the confidentiality of data Preserve the integrity of data Promote the availability of data for authorized use Trust by vendors and users. (Are you limiting YOUR online activities because you no longer fully trust the security of online systems?)

docs.title: Slide 10
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

10

docs.title: Slide 11
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Confidentiality

11

Confidentiality is sometimes referred to as the principle of least privilege, meaning that users should be given only enough privilege to perform their duties, and no more. Common synonyms for confidentiality include privacy, secrecy, and discretion.

docs.title: Slide 12
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Confidentiality

12

Confidentiality models are intended to block unauthorized access to information and make sure accidental disclosure of sensitive information is not possible. Common confidentiality controls are user IDs and passwords, 2 factor verification 2FV.

docs.title: Slide 13
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Integrity models

13

Integrity models keep data pure and trustworthy by protecting system data from intentional or accidental changes. Integrity models have three goals: Prevent unauthorized users from making modifications to data or programs Prevent authorized users from making improper or unauthorized modifications Maintain internal and external consistency of data and programs

docs.title: Slide 14
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Integrity models

An example of integrity checks is balancing a batch of transactions to make sure that all the information is present and accurately accounted for.

14

docs.title: Slide 15
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Availability models

Availability models keep data and resources available for authorized use, especially during emergencies or disasters.

15

docs.title: Slide 16
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Availability models

16

Three common challenges to availability: Denial of service (DoS) due to intentional attacks or because of undiscovered flaws in implementation (for example, a program written by a programmer who is unaware of a flaw that could crash the program if a certain unexpected input is encountered) Loss of information system capabilities because of natural disasters (fires, floods, storms, or earthquakes) or human actions (bombs or strikes) Equipment failures during normal use

docs.title: Slide 17
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

CIA Triad

17

What is the CIA Triad - IBM technology video – 4 mins.

docs.text:

Sources of threat

What do you believe are the most likely threats to information security?

18

docs.title: Slide 19
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Phishing

19

“Phishing is the fraudulent attempt to obtain sensitive information such as usernames, passwords and credit card details by disguising as a trustworthy entity in an electronic communication”

docs.title: Slide 20
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Phishing

20

Search for these three examples of successful Phishing attacks: Mr Walter Stephan, CEO of FACC Seagate employees W-2 tax records Free FIFA World Cup Tickets

docs.text:

https://phishingquiz.withgoogle.com/

Quiz challenge

Hacking

White Hat: The Good Kind Black Hat: The Not Good Kind Hacktivist: “Thinks They’re Doing Good Kind” as well as many more.

22

docs.title: Slide 23
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Hacking may only be one part of a broader cyber attack

23

docs.title: Slide 24
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Bots

24

Bots are micro programs that can be designed to scan a system to find specific information. Bots are used widely for both good and bad Cyber criminals use bots to find credit card information, weak points in new software patches or previously unknown access points that can then be exploited. They are used in the early stages of an attack to locate the simplest method of access.

docs.title: Slide 25
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Compliance with Cyber Security Policies

25

Does your workplace allow any of the following: signing into personal social media accounts from work machines? emailing sensitive data to work from a home machine or such data to your home machine? connecting company devices to unsecured networks? storing passwords on Word documents?

docs.title: Slide 26
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Misuse Of Employee Privileges

26

The most common issues tend to be company devices being used for non-work purposes or else for work but in insecure locations. Preventative actions: blocking access to websites that pose a potential threat through malware or phishing, ensuring administrator privileges remain with the IT department, automated monitoring of device usage

docs.title: Slide 27
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

BYOD (Bring Your Own Device)

27

A study conducted by HP identified that 97% of employees’ devices contained privacy issues and 75% did not have sufficient data encryption. Storage devices;

laptops, mobile phones, tablets and even wearable technology

docs.title: Slide 28
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Staff training

28

Training ALL staff to be cyber aware and compliant with cyber security policies and procedures is essential. Provide ALL teams with the right skills to deal with potential threats before they mature into an imminent risk to your systems integrity.

docs.title: Slide 29
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

“Disaster” Recovery Planning

29

Assume one day the organization will suffer a successful cyber-attack. Post attack - obtain data from all network logs, error reports and personal observations that could indicate the cause of the data breach. Then review the data breach itself: What preventative measures were put in place to stop it What immediately followed the termination of the threat.

docs.title: Slide 30
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Password Cracking

30

Generally accepted guidance when creating a strong password include: Don’t use the same password over and over for different applications. Avoid information about your family (for example: names or date of birth) Ensure all passwords are significantly different by adding unusual symbols.

docs.title: Slide 31
docs.text:
OFFICIAL

Ransomware

Software designed to block access to a machine and files until a sum of money is paid. Ensure that critical files are backed up either continuously or very frequently. Keep your backup activity diversified and encrypt it so it makes unauthorized access harder to accomplish.

31

docs.text:
